name: Possessive Articles
summary: Say my, your, his, and her with words that behave just like ein.
explanation:
Possessive articles show ownership: mein (my), dein (your, informal), sein (his/its), ihr (her/their), unser (our), euer (your, plural), Ihr (your, formal).

The good news: they all take exactly the same endings as 'ein' and 'kein'. So they change for the gender and case of the thing owned, not the owner. 'mein Bruder' (my brother, masculine), 'meine Schwester' (my sister, feminine), 'mein Auto' (my car, neuter), 'meine Eltern' (my parents, plural).

In the accusative, only the masculine adds -en: 'Ich sehe meinen Bruder' (I see my brother).

examples.en: She is looking for her key. (masculine accusative)

commonMistakes: Matching the possessive to the owner instead of the owned noun: it is 'meine Schwester' because Schwester is feminine.
Forgetting the -e for feminine and plural: it is 'meine Mutter', not 'mein Mutter'.
Confusing 'ihr' (her/their) with 'Ihr' (formal your). Capitalisation carries the meaning.
Leaving masculine unmarked in the accusative: it is 'Ich sehe meinen Vater'.
Adding endings to the wrong stem with 'euer', which drops an e: 'eure Kinder', not 'euere Kinder'.
tip: If you can already use ein and kein, you can use every possessive. They share one ending system.
